corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

When the size of the support set exceeds a certain limit, it can become difficult to leverage all the examples with in-context learning: first because it becomes excessively expensive to fit all the examples in the prompt, and second because there is a risk of poor generalization when the prompt size exceeds the size of the sequence used during training .

When provided access to a large-scale task-specific dataset with many thousands of examples, we find that we can improve results over our previously presented in-context few-shot learning results, setting a new state of the art on five tasks: VQAv2, VATEX, VizWiz, MSRVTTQA, and HatefulMemes.
For example, on VQAv2, we observe improved results at 82.0%, outperforming our results achieved with 32-shot in-context learning (67.3%) as well as the previous state of the art (81.3%, Yan et al. ).

In some cases our results likely trail the state of the art due in part to the fact that we simply optimise log-likelihood and do not make use of common task-specific metric optimisation tricks, such as CIDEr optimisation for COCO captioning, and fine-tuning on dense annotations for VisDial .

Despite being a smaller dataset ALIGN by a factor of 6, a contrastive model trained on only LTIP outperforms one trained only on ALIGN on our evaluation metrics, suggesting that dataset quality may be more important than scale in the regimes in which we operate.

Across all evaluation metrics, we find that the Accumulation method outperforms other methods of combining the datasets.
